dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for unconscious patient at 1500 Block of Clarkson Road, [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened here today?
My partner found him unconscious in the parking lot about five minutes ago.
Was the patient breathing, and were there any movements or responses noted before he collapsed?
He was breathing but not responding. He had abnormal breathing and a blank stare, then went limp again.
Let's check the patient's responsiveness before we proceed further.
Eye-opening is spontaneous. He is not fully alert, so we'll assess GCS step by step.
Patient does not respond appropriately to loud voice or pain stimulation.
GCS motor response is localizing to pain. Total GCS is 12.
Airway is patent and free from obstruction.
Spontaneous breathing, shallow and irregular. We'll get pulse oximetry right away.
SpO2 is 98%. Respirations are 24. Breathing remains shallow and irregular.
Pulse is 105, BP is 192 over 114. No active bleeding. He is hypertensive.
GCS is 12 during primary assessment. He is showing signs of a postictal state with seizure-like movements.
Ma'am, can you tell us what happened just before your partner collapsed?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: He was fine at first, then started moving erratically, like a deer in headlights, and collapsed.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: How long did the episode last?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: It seems like about 7 minutes. Then he stopped on his own.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Was he on any medications or previously known to have seizures?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No, he never reported any health issues or seizure history.
Since he is unconscious, the pain level is assumed to be 0.
Skin is warm, dry, pink, with normal turgor. No rashes or hives observed. No pallor noted.
Pupils are equal and reactive-at about 5 mm. No JVD or tracheal deviation. No trauma.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: No signs of trauma or obstruction in the ears, nose, or throat.
Chest is symmetric, no deformities or signs of injury. Atraumatic.
Pulse is now 99. Slightly decreased from initial check.
Respiratory rate is 20. Breathing appears more regular now.
Current BP is 136 over 88. Vital signs are showing some improvement.
Let's run a 4-lead EKG to get more details about cardiac rhythm.
IV was successfully started in the left arm on the first attempt.
Administering Midazolam (Versed) 5 mg IV to help control seizure activity.
Obtaining a 12-lead ECG now and rechecking oxygen saturation.
Blood glucose is reading 0. We'll make a note and monitor for potential hypoglycemia.
SpO2 is now at 94%. We're watching for a potential decline.
Based on signs of possible seizure and altered mental status, we are following protocol 3-12-medical seizure protocol.
GCS has improved to 14-patient is showing signs of regaining consciousness.
EKG is now sinus rhythm. Heart activity is stable.
Given the location and his current status, [COMPANY_NAME] is the nearest appropriate facility.
Preparing to transfer using a two-person carry for safe transport.
Patient is ready. Equipment and accessories are in place.
Patient is now on the stretcher. Begin securing and preparing for transport.
ETCO2 nasal cannula is active, monitoring breathing. All transport prep is complete.
Arriving with a 48-year-old male who had a possible absence seizure and is in a postictal state.
Pt is alert and oriented x2/4. Vital signs are stable, IV intact. Midazolam administered. All transport events uneventful.
Pupils are now assessed as 3 mm and PEARL. Re-evaluated after medications.
ETCO2 monitoring has been initiated to continue respiratory monitoring on transport.
Initial EKG reading was sinus tachycardia with nonspecific T wave changes.
No signs of trauma to the torso or back. Skin turgor and texture remain normal.
No signs of nuchal rigidity or brain injury. Pupils noted earlier at 5 mm, now 3 mm.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: Oral cavity free from obstruction. No swelling or foreign bodies identified.
Bilateral breath sounds present with no adventitious sounds.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender with normal bowel sounds.
No deformity or angulation noted to the extremities or spine.
Midazolam administration had a positive therapeutic response-no side effects observed.
There is no reported current medications or allergies.
He had breakfast about two hours before this occurred.
No known past medical history or chronic conditions.
Rechecking IV site for patency every 10 minutes to prevent complications.
Monitoring continues with hourly vital updates while enroute and post-arrival.
Pain level remains 0 per ROS and Glasgow Coma Scale observation.
No changes in medical history, medications, or allergies reported.
Stretcher secured properly in the ambulance. Ready for swift and smooth travel.
All equipment including IV pump, EKG, and ETCO2 device operational for monitoring enroute.
[CITY] is receiving. No alternate hospital transfers planned at this time.
In transport, patient maintains normal BP and weaker pulse but stable.
Most recent EKG showed a return to sinus rhythm with sinus tach resolved.
All criteria from protocol 3-12 have been followed thoroughly.
Bag-valve-mask not in current use; patient breathing normally on their own.
Patient stable and secured for transport. No incidents since arrival on scene.
Postictal state is apparent. No neurological deterioration observed.
Continuing to watch the SpO2 trend for steady values enroute.
Rechecking stretcher restraints for safety and ensuring no accidental movement.
Blood pressure stable with stabilized heart rhythms and no emergency signs.
Ambulance heading directly to [CITY] with lateral and longitudinal restraints in place.
Patient's family member is accompanying him and is prepared for hospital intake.
Arrival expected in 15 minutes after passing two intersections and far entry road.
No additional IV fluids or medications currently required in-line.
All transmission equipment is secured to reduce vibration and false readings.
Current vitals in normal range. GCS remains stable at 14 enroute.
Reporting progress every 5 minutes in case of any field changes reported.
Paperwork, patient name, DOB, and addresses confirmed for hospital transfer.
IV fluid status stable and communication logs updated for all field changes.
Transport alarm set for arrival, pre-trip inspection verified at dispatch departure.
Comfort position maintained with reduced immobilization to prevent stiffness.
Midazolam not to be repeated due to protocol limitations until hospital control.
All area exits on route accounted in case of traffic detours or complications.
Curb approach verified at [CITY]-wheelchair accessible door cleared.
Final skin check shows no burns, rashes, swelling, or unnoted trauma.
Emergency contact dB and family informed of events and arrival expected time.
Patient, family, and paperwork all accounted after incident and final prep to leave scene.
Head injury high on protocol but unremarkable found upon complete exam.
Final verbal sign-off from receiving hospital given for full facilities readiness.
Air, ground, or fly sections per transport policy-terrestrial abbreviated with vehicle use.
All equipment discharged from stretcher area for efficient room availability.
Midazolam not to be repeated due to protocol limitations until hospital control.
Lower back and limb checks reaffirmed for compatibility to be moved safely.
Hospital report includes non-verbal response to pain, verbal face-washing action, arms reaching.
[CITY] anticipates arrival with a midazolam-reponsive seizure patient with ETCO2 tracking.
Patient has been secured and monitored. Transport will proceed without incident.
Ensuring first responders at [CITY] are alerted and prepared for arrival.
